draft.introduction: Chandogya Upanishad 3.14 presents the meditation on Brahman as dwelling within the heart, smaller than a grain of rice yet greater than the entire cosmos. It is one of the Upanishads’ most influential passages on the identity of the individual self (atman) with the universal reality (Brahman).

translation.text: All this is Brahman. One should meditate on that with tranquility. This self of mine within the heart is smaller than a grain of rice, smaller than a mustard seed, smaller than a grain of millet. This self of mine within the heart is greater than the earth, greater than the sky, greater than the heavens, greater than all the worlds. This is Brahman.

translator_notes.note: The paradox of Brahman as simultaneously smaller than the smallest and greater than the greatest expresses the Upanishadic insight that the infinite is not merely vast but intimately present. This teaching directly influenced Shankara’s Advaita Vedanta and bears remarkable similarity to Nicholas of Cusa’s coincidence of opposites.
